Introduction
Document scope
1.1 Document scope
The corrective maintenance manual (CMM) helps service engineers troubleshoot, repair and
maintain the systems, subsystems and components of the linear accelerator. The target audience
is qualified service engineers. These may be engineers employed directly by Elekta, qualified
representatives of Elekta or qualified representatives of Elekta's customers.
The manual provides:
• A description of the system or sub-system in question.
• A troubleshooting section to identify the problem areas.
• A set of detailed procedures used to replace FRUs (field replaceable units).
• A section on how to return the device to work.
If you find any mistakes in this document, or identify areas for improvement, please raise a CLM
(customer lifecycle management) case and assign it to the relevant queue.
1.2 Intended users
Table 1.1 Definition of persons who can use or work on the equipment
Term Definition
Authorized A person that is permitted by the user to use or do work on the equipment by
person the User of the equipment.
Note: The authorized person must have the correct level of knowledge and
training before using or doing work on the equipment.
A person that is approved by Elekta to have the necessary knowledge and
Elekta approved training to do specified tasks on Elekta equipment.
person Note: The authorized person must have the correct level of knowledge and
training before using or doing work on the equipment.
Operator A person who operates the equipment with or without help from an assistant,
and who controls some or all the functions of the equipment.
Note: The operator must have the correct level of knowledge and training
before using or doing work on the equipment.
Clinical User A person who operates the equipment for the treatment of patients.
Note: A Clinical User must be a qualified person who has the necessary
knowledge and training in the safe, clinical operation of the equipment. Such
treatment must be therapeutic only.
Clinical User – The role of Radiation Therapists, also referred to as Radiotherapists, Radiation
Radiation Treatment Technicians or Therapeutic/Therapy radiographers, may be
Therapist different based on their geographical region and experience/qualifications.
However, their primary roles and obligations are expected to be similar among
different regions and sites.
Note: Radiation Therapists are trained on all aspects of system operation, but
not trained on installation, calibration or service maintenance.
Note: Radiation Therapists who work at the site when the system is installed
will be trained by an Elekta trainer. After the system is introduced, Elekta
authorized trainers, or experienced Radiation Therapists who are already
trained on the equipment, can train Radiation Therapists who are new to the
equipment.

Introduction
Intended users
Term Definition
Clinical User – Dosimetrists design, generate, and measure radiation dose distributions and
Medical dose calculations in collaboration with the Medical Physics Expert and
Dosimetrist Radiation Oncologist.
Note: Dosimetrists are trained on the general principles of system operation,
but not trained on installation, calibration, or service maintenance.
Medical Physics A person that is recognized by a competent authority to have the necessary
Expert (MPE) knowledge and training in radiation physics, or radiation treatment, to give
Radiation information on patient dosimetry, use of radiation procedures and equipment,
Protection on optimization, quality assurance, do the applicable tests and calculations for
Advisor (RPA) radiation levels and dose assessments, and other items related to medical
radiation exposure and safety.
Note: Medical Physicists are trained on performing system QA procedures and
calibration or service maintenance tasks.
Radiation A physician whose trained medical specialty is the use of radiation therapy as
Oncologist a treatment for cancer.
Radiotherapy A Nurse with specific focus on the Radiation Oncology clinic patient support.
Nurse Certain countries utilize specially trained nurses to run the Linear Accelerator.
Qualified person A person that is recognized by a competent authority to have the necessary
knowledge and training to do specified tasks.
Note: The qualified person must have the correct level of knowledge and
training before using or doing work on the equipment.
Qualified Expert A person that is recognized by a competent authority to do the applicable
(QE) tests and calculations for radiation levels and dose assessments. The person
also provides information on the necessary correct radiation protection and
procedures.
Note: The qualified expert must have the correct level of knowledge and
training before using or doing work on the equipment.
Service User - A Service User operates the equipment to do maintenance tasks. A Service
Service engineer User is a qualified person who has the necessary knowledge and training to
set different software configurations, do the necessary tests, adjustments,
optimization, and calibration procedures on the equipment. Such operation
must not be therapeutic.
Note: The Service User must have the correct level of knowledge and training
before using or doing work on the equipment.
User The organization or person responsible for the operation and maintenance of
the equipment.
Note: The user must have the correct level of knowledge and training before
using or doing work on the equipment.
For more information on persons that can work on the equipment, and on training, contact your
local Elekta representative.

1.4 Glossary of terms
Table 1.3 Glossary of terms
Term Definition
Autotrack function The autotrack function automatically moves the diaphragms or
leaves to their prescribed position.
Cal block The Cal block contains the item and part values that, when
loaded, configure the linear accelerator for a given energy.
Clinical Mode In Clinical Mode, Clinical Users use the linear accelerator and its
accessories for the radiation therapy treatment that a licensed
medical practitioner prescribes.
Gating Gating is the procedure by which an external system or manual
task pauses and starts the radiation delivery as a result of
information received relating to the treatment position of the
patient.
Interdigitation Interdigitation is an overlap of the opposite adjacent leaves of a
multileaf collimator.
Service life Service life is the length of time that the regular servicing and
repair procedures can keep the machine in operation.
Service Mode In Service Mode, Service Users do the necessary maintenance
tasks and calibration of the equipment.

General safety information
Important safety instructions
2.1 Important safety instructions
It is important to install, operate, maintain, and dispose of the equipment according to the
information in the product documentation, and in compliance with local regulations. For the
safety of the users and the patients, before a user operates or works on the equipment, they
must read, understand, and obey all:
• Warnings, cautions, instructions, and notes in this document and the related
documents.
• Safety instructions, emergency procedures, and information in this document and the
related documents.
• Warnings, cautions, and safety markings on the equipment and the accessories of the
equipment.
If you have local work instructions for the equipment, then Elekta recommends that you include
the warnings, cautions, and notes from this document in your local work instructions.
2.1.1 Conventions for warnings, cautions, and notes
The conventions give information about the class and the level of the risk in the warnings and
cautions, and the content of notes.
WARNING 2.1
A warning is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions with a risk of fatal or serious
injury, injury, or clinical mistreatment, if you do not obey the instructions.
CAUTION 2.1
A caution is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions with a risk of damage to the
equipment, data, or the environment, if you do not obey the instructions.
Note: A note gives more information about a related text.
2.1.2 Expected service life of the equipment
The expected service life is the length of time that the regular service and repair procedures can
keep the equipment safe to operate.
WARNING 2.2
Do not operate the equipment unless you correctly complete the safety checks, planned
maintenance schedules, and extended life checks. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
The expected service life of this equipment is 15 years of normal use.
The Elekta definition for 'expected service life' is the length of time that the regular service and
repair procedures can keep the equipment safe to operate.
The Elekta definition of 'normal use' is the treatment of 50 patients each day for five days each
week of the year. If the equipment is used more frequently than this, the frequency of the
planned maintenance must increase accordingly and the extended life checks must also be
brought forward accordingly.

General safety information
Important safety instructions
The level of operation of the equipment can be different to ‘normal use’. This has an effect on the
length of the service life. Because of wear of parts, the risk of a malfunction can increase after 10
years operation. Elekta recommends that before you continue operation after 10 years, you
complete the regular extended life checks.
Elekta can help the User with the correct safety checks, planned maintenance schedules, and the
regular extended life checks for continued safe operation of the equipment.
The system must be maintained throughout its expected service life in compliance with the EMC
information detailed in the applicable Instructions for Use.
For information on the above checks and schedules, contact your local Elekta representative.

2.2 Safety precautions
2.2.1 Safety precautions for the compatibility of the equipment
WARNING 2.3
Do not use parts, accessories, or other equipment, without approval by Elekta of their
compatibility. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury and damage to the
equipment.
Only operate the equipment with Elekta supplied or approved, compatible equipment or parts.
Accessories, transducers, and cables that are not specified by or do not have the approval of
Elekta can cause an incorrect treatment delivery. They can also have an effect on the performance
of the electromagnetic compatibility (EMC), which can increase the emissions or decrease the
immunity of the equipment.
Contact Elekta for information about the compatibility of other equipment or parts.
2.2.2 Safety precautions for the electromagnetic compatibility
The design and manufacture of Elekta products is in compliance with applicable EMC standards.
The intended result of the standards is to minimize the risk of electromagnetic interference by RF
devices. The type of device and the distance to the equipment has an effect on the intensity of
the electromagnetic interference. The equipment includes the linear accelerator and accessories,
the control cabinets, the computers in the control room, and equipment from other
manufacturer.

General safety information
Safety precautions
It is necessary to have special precautions for EMC emissions. Elekta recommends that you obey
these precautions when you use RF devices near the equipment.
• Do not use RF devices unless they are in compliance with international standards for
electromagnetic compatibility, for example, the CE or FCC mark.
• Do not use a two-way emergency radio in the treatment room or the control room.
Only use an emergency radio if there is an emergency.
• Do not use cellular telephones, or other devices with cellular modems near the
equipment. The Instructions for Use contains information on the minimum safe
distance of these devices from the equipment.
• When you use a device that is enabled for wireless operation, it must be a minimum of
10 cm from the equipment.
• Radio-Frequency Identification (RFID) readers, when activated to do a scan of a RFID
tag, must be a minimum of one meter from the equipment.
Note: The RFID systems that Elekta supply use lower power levels. You can safely use these RFID systems
when you obey the procedures in the Instructions for Use for the product.
The system must be installed, put into service, and maintained in compliance with the EMC
information in the Instructions for Use.
Contact Elekta for information about the compatibility of parts and equipment.
WARNING 2.4
Do not put portable or mobile radio frequency (RF) communication devices near to the
equipment. The emissions from such RF devices can be more than the EMC standards, and can
have unwanted effects on the equipment operation. If you ignore this warning, the RF
emissions can cause the equipment to have a malfunction. A malfunction can cause fatal injury,
clinical mistreatment, or damage to the equipment.
WARNING 2.5
Do not use accessories, transducers, and cables that are not specified by Elekta. They can have
an effect on the EMC performance, which can increase EMC emissions or decrease the
immunity to RF interference of the equipment. If you ignore this warning, incorrect radiation
delivery and other malfunctions can occur, which can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.
2.2.3 Safety precaution for Service Tools and lifting equipment
WARNING 2.6
Do not use defective or damaged lifting equipment. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
WARNING 2.7
Do not use incorrectly calibrated or defective Service Tools. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause injury and damage to the equipment.
Before you use the Service Tool or lifting equipment, make sure that:
• There are no defective or damaged parts
• The calibration of the Service Tool is correct

For information on the maintenance and calibration schedules of the Service Tool, see the
Instructions for Use and Calibration Certificate for the tool.
2.2.4 Safety precaution for work with electrical and mechanical parts
WARNING 2.8
Do not connect any electrical power supply before the ground earth connections are secure.
There is a risk of high leakage current. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 2.9
Do not remove the cables or the covers, or operate the equipment without the covers. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury
The covers on the equipment prevent access to high voltage parts and parts that move. If you
remove the covers, there is a risk of high voltage shock or injury from parts that move.
Only remove the covers if you are an Elekta approved person with the correct authority and you
obey the applicable safety procedures.
2.2.5 Safety precaution for work with safety devices during service
operation
WARNING 2.10
Do not remove, change, or override the safety switches, interlocks, and other safety devices. If
you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.
The procedures in this document can tell you to remove, change, or override the safety switches,
interlocks, or other safety devices during repair or maintenance work. When the work is
completed, make all the safety devices active again, and do a test of them.
2.2.6 Safety precaution for work on BLDs with UV lighting
WARNING 2.11
Do not look into the ultraviolet lighting of the BLD without protective glasses. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause serious injury.
The covers and screens of the BLD prevent an injury to your eyes from the ultraviolet lighting.
Before you remove the covers or screens of the BLD, switch off the linear accelerator, or use
protective glasses. Protective glasses must have the UV-A specification OD 6+ @ 190 - 532 Nm.
If there is damage to the covers or screen of the BLD, switch off the linear accelerator and contact
the Elekta representative.

General safety information
Safety precautions
2.2.7 Safety precaution for work with lasers
WARNING 2.12
Do not look into the laser beams because they can cause damage to your eyes. If you ignore
this warning, you can cause serious injury.
Elekta systems use the laser beams to help set up patients in the correct position for treatment.
During your work in the treatment room, make sure that you do not look into the laser beams.
2.2.8 Safety precautions for work with display screen equipment
WARNING 2.13
Do not use the display screen equipment for long periods of time. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause injury.

2.2.14 Safety precaution for the removal of parts from the radiation head
WARNING 2.21

ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
After the operation of the linear accelerator at high energy, the parts in the radiation head can
become radioactive and a radiation hazard. Before you do work on, or remove parts from the
radiation head, use a calibrated radiation detector to measure the level of induced radioactivity.
Use the applicable safety procedures when you remove radioactive parts and material. The
maximum dose rate for the safe removal of radioactive parts and material is 50 µSv/hr
(microsievert per hour). Do not remove the radioactive parts and material from the treatment
room before they are correctly prepared for safe removal.

decommissioning
WARNING 2.22
Do not do work, or make modifications to the equipment without the correct training and
authority. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, and
damage to the equipment.
Only do work on, or make modifications to, the equipment if you are an Elekta approved person
with the applicable authority. Such work must use best practice and obey the laws and
regulations in the jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed.

approved person with the correct authority, can cause:
• Fatal injury
• Incorrect radiation delivery
• Malfunction of the equipment
• Damage to the equipment
• The warranty to be canceled.

radiation protection functions, devices, systems, procedures, and accessories. Always obey the

equipment is installed.

2.2.16.1 Safety precautions for the measurement of induced radioactivity
The safety precautions for the measurement of induced radioactivity are the necessary safety
precautions to know to prevent excessive exposure to radioactivity, which can cause serious
injury or damage to the environment.
Always use two radiation detectors to measure the level of induced radioactivity. Only use
radiation detectors that have an applicable calibration certificate. The period of time since the
last calibration date must be less than 12 months. Do not use the radiation detectors if the period
of time since the last calibration date is more than 12 months.
WARNING 2.23
Do not move radioactive material (by air, sea, or land) unless you obey the applicable laws and
regulations for induced radioactivity and disposal of radioactive material of the jurisdictions
through which the movement occurs. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury
and damage to the environment.
2.2.16.2 Safety precautions for handling parts with induced radioactivity
The handling of parts with induced radioactivity must comply with the laws and regulations of the
jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed. The safe levels of radiation applicable in the UK
do not apply in all jurisdictions. The permitted level of radioactivity must always be as low as
reasonably achievable (ALARA). This level may vary depending on local rules, therefore the safe
working levels from the applicable local rules must be considered as the acceptable working limit.
If there are no safe levels available for a jurisdiction, use the UK value as a guideline, which is a
maximum of 150 cps at the surface for a maximum duration of 72 minutes. The following table
shows the conversion from this value to other units.

Product description
Overview of the power supplies and interlocks
4.1 Overview of the power supplies and interlocks
The digital accelerator uses high voltages, makes hazardous radiation, and has parts that move
automatically. For the protection of patients and other persons near to the machine, the control
system of the digital accelerator has a number of safety circuits. The safety circuits operate
different interlocks that prevent the operation of the digital accelerator if there are hazardous
conditions.
4.2 Description of the electrical power supplies of the linear
accelerator
The linear accelerator supplies electrical power for a number of user systems and subsystems.
The user systems are the:
• Three-phase electrical power supply
• Low voltage power supplies (high current)
• Low tension (LT) power supplies (low power)
• Interlock system.
Related Links:

4.3 Description of the electrical power supplies to the linear
accelerator
The linear accelerator connects to three-phase electrical supplies from the hospital electrical
power supply. The electrical supplies are:
• 220 VAC to 250 V AC, or
• 380 VAC to 440 VAC.
The linear accelerator gets its primary three-phase electrical supply through an isolator in the
control room. The isolator sets the electrical power on and off.
The isolator supplies the electrical power supply to the reeling interface cabinet (RIC). The RIC
contains the circuit breakers and contactors, through which the electrical supplies go to the
different user systems.

Product description
Description of the mains power supply connections in the RIC
4.4 Description of the mains power supply connections in the
RIC
The three-phase mains power supply to the reeling interface cabinet (RIC) connects to the screw
connectors on terminal block TS73A in Area 73. The connectors are:
• TS73A-34 for phase 1 (A)
• TS73A-35 for phase 2 (B)
• TS73A-36 for phase 3 (C)
• TS73A-37 for neutral (N).
Each of the line fuses has a fuse rating of 40 A.
The primary ground (earth) connection of the mains power supply connects to ground (earth)
terminal 1 (ET1) in the bottom of the RIC.
4.5 Description of the client interface supplies
The client interface supplies are customer specified power supplies. The reeling interface cabinet
(RIC) sends the client interface supplies through a mains distribution unit (MDU) to the treatment
control cabinet (TCC). The TCC supplies the client interface supplies to the related user systems,
which are the:
• Vacuum system
• Room lights
• Wall lasers
• Ready alarm circuit
• Radiation alarm circuit
• External water cooler remote start.
A stand-alone uninterruptible power supply (UPS) or the MDU supply power to the peripheral
equipment in the TCC.
Note: Some of the subsystems get their power supply through the client interface which is not part of
the three phase electrical supply. Some of the subsystems can have power applied when the three
phase isolator is set to OFF.
4.6 Description of the low voltage power supply unit
The LV PSU is in Area 13 on the gantry. A thyristor controls the operation of the LV PSU to supply a
constant current DC power supply. The LV PSU supplies current for the focus, bending magnet and
magnetron magnet systems. The LT contactor system controls the mains power supply to the
LV PSU.

When the contactors energize, the contacts of the contactors close. The closed contacts of the

If the system operates correctly:
• The hardware interlock chains send a +24 V signal to energize the related contactors.
• Each hardware interlock chain has a number of directly connected switches and relay
contacts in the +24 V supply line to the related contactor.
• Each switch or relay is set by an interlock condition (for example, EMERGENCY STOP).
• When all the interlock conditions are set, the switch and relay contacts in the interlock
chain stay closed to supply +24 V to the contactor.
If an interlock fault condition occurs:
• The monitor circuit senses the condition.
• The monitor circuit sends a signal which opens the contacts of the related switch or
relay. This breaks the interlock chain and removes the +24 V power supply from the
related contactor.
• The contacts of the contactor open to remove the mains power supply from the
related system.
4.8.2.1 Description of the main interlock circuit
The main interlock circuit controls the 24 V power supply to the main system contactor, CON-A.
This is the first device in a chain of contactors which are connected in series with the three-phase
mains power supply. When only CON-A is energized, the machine state is always System On.
The EMERGENCY OFF switches in the treatment room and the control room are part of the main
interlock circuit. Operation of an EMERGENCY OFF switch removes the three-phase mains power
supply from the digital accelerator.

4.8.2.2 Description of the LT interlocks
The LT interlocks circuit controls the low voltage, three-phase power supplies of the digital
accelerator. The LT interlock operates LT contactors CON-F and CON-H. The contacts of these are
connected in series with the mains power supply to the LV PSU. When the contactors CON-F,

4.8.2.3 Functional description of the movements interlock
The movements interlock controls the supply of the single-phase mains power supply to the
motors of the movement systems. The movements interlock has a primary interlock circuit and a
secondary interlock circuit.
The primary movements interlock operates the contactor CON-B. The STOP button on the
handheld controller (HHC) and the movement stop switches control the operation of the primary
movements interlock. The secondary movements interlock operates the contactor CON-C. The
touchguards control the operation of the secondary movements interlock.
Contactors CON-B and CON-C are connected in series, and control the 26 V power supplies to the
movement system motors. When the digital accelerator is in the Preparatory machine state, CON-
C energizes. The closed contacts of CON-C send a 26 V power supply to the secondary movements
interlock. When CON-B energizes, the movements system interlock sends 26 V power supplies to
the movement system motors.
If a dangerous condition occurs, or the emergency shut down relay in the TCC operates, the
contacts of the contactors open. The open contacts of the contactors break the movements
interlock chain. This removes the 26 V power supply from the movement system motors and
stops movements.

4.8.2.4 Description of the HT interlock
The HT interlocks control the distribution of the three-phase mains power supply to the HT PSU.
The HT interlock has a dual circuit which applies the mains power supply to the HT PSU:
• The HT1 (primary) interlock circuit operates contactor CON-J.
• The HT2 (secondary) interlock circuit operates contactor CON-K.
Each contactor is connected in series with the three-phase mains power supply. The contactors
must be energized or set before they can apply the mains power supply to the HT PSU. When the
HT contactors are all energized, the machine state of the digital accelerator changes to
Intersegment.
An HT soft-start circuit decreases the mains voltage for a while before it applies full power to the
HT PSU.
The HT interlocks set these conditions for operation:
• The room doors are closed
• The vacuum pressure is correct
• The dosimetry system parameters for operation are correct
• The collimators and filters in the BLD are in the correct position.
• All the software conditions necessary to apply the HT supply are correct.
• The START, TERMINATE and INTERRUPT keys on the hand held controllers (HHCs) are
correctly set.
• The control area RTUs are in the correct condition.

4.8.2.5 Description of the PRF interlock
The PRF interlock monitors the thyratron trigger pulses sent to the RF subsystem (see Figure 4.2).
The PRF interlock has two circuits:
• PRF A monitors the RTUs on the A-side of the Serial communications link
• PRF B monitors the RTUs on the B-side of the Serial communications link.
If a dangerous condition occurs, the PRF interlock stops the transmission of thyratron trigger
pulses to the RF subsystem. When the PRF interlock chain is completed, the machine state of the
digital accelerator changes to Radiation.

4.8.3 Description of the firmware interlocks
The PLDs on the DIE PCBs in each control area have firmware interlocks. The firmware interlocks
operate the relays in the hardware interlock chains. They also compare the condition of the digital
accelerator with each other, and against other values. This makes sure that the conditions are
compatible.
The DIE PCBs each have two PLDs. Each PLD receives a control code of the digital accelerator from
the TCS. Each PLD also receives a control code from the TCS. The TCS monitors the status of each
PLD to make sure that the control code is valid. If the control code is not valid, the digital
accelerator closes down.
4.8.4 Description of the software interlocks
The TCS software interlocks operate to examine the condition of other interlocks (see Table 4.6).
A minimum of one relay in each interlock chain cannot close until the TCS sends a contactor on
command. The TCS continuously makes sure that all the digital accelerator conditions are safe
and compatible. This is a duplicate activity to the hardware and firmware interlock operations.
The software interlocks monitor and control the:
• Radiation setting
• Dosimetry
• Performance verification
• Digital accelerator protection.

4.9 System description of the power supplies and interlocks
4.9.1 Operation and analysis of the low voltage power supply unit
The LV PSU assembly has three transformers (T1, T2, and T3). T1 is the high power transformer.
The transformers supply decreased voltage inputs to five different power supply circuits on the
assembly, which are the:
• Bending coarse current to the bending magnet in the beam physics system
• Bending fine current to the bending magnet in the beam physics system
• Focus 1 coil in the beam physics system
• Focus 2 coil in the beam physics system
• Magnetron magnet in the RF system.
Apart from the bending coarse current, each LV PSU circuit is electrically the same. Each LV PSU
circuit has:
• A thyristor array
• A control PCB
• A feedback shunt resistor
• A circuit to smooth the output.
A secondary winding of T1 supplies power to the LV PSU circuit. A secondary winding of T3
supplies 15 V AC (rms) power supply for the control PCB in the circuit.
The control PCB receives a command signal from the related TCS control area to supply trigger
pulses to control the thyristor gates.
Each LV PSU circuit also supplies:
• The solenoid power supply
• A sample of the positive output voltage
• A current monitor signal, from the feedback voltage of the circuit shunt resistor.
The bending coarse current controls a three-phase supply, and therefore, uses three different
control PCBs. The TCS controls the PCB that has the positive output line (PCB 13C). T1 applies
three different 11.5 V power supplies to three related pairs of 80 A in parallel fuses (FS13A, FS13B
and FS13C). This gives a 160 A current on each supply line. T2 supplies 15 V AC (rms) to the
control PCBs of the bending coarse current.
The parts of the LV PSU assembly are on a heat sink assembly that has a cooling fan to control the
temperature. T1 also has protection against high temperatures. Each positive supply line from the
secondary windings of T2 and T3 includes a positive temperature coefficient (PTC) device. This
gives the transformers protection against high currents.
Refer to the Elekta Digital Accelerator Systems Diagrams 4513 330 7057.
Refer to the Elekta Oncology Products Service Parts Catalog for a diagram of the LV PSU assembly.

There are two types of control PCB for the LV PSU:
• Type A, has a control signal input and a current monitor circuit.
• Type B is almost the same as type A, but does not have the control input and monitor
circuit.

The control PCB of the LV PSU controls the operation of a thyristor, which is a SCR. The SCR

4.9.4 Operation and analysis of an emergency shutdown by the TCS
The TCS software controls the operation of the two emergency shutdown relays in the TCC. The
operation of only one of the emergency shutdown relays is necessary to cause an emergency
shutdown of the digital accelerator.
Operation of an emergency stop switch sends an emergency shutdown signal to the TCS. The TCS
sends a shutdown signal to the emergency shutdown relays in the TCC. The contacts of the
emergency shutdown relays de-energize to remove the +22 V power supply to the digital
accelerator. The hardware interlock and PRF interlock circuits de-energize, which:
• Stop radiation
• Disable the HT power supplies
• Stop movements.

4.9.8 Operation and analysis of the secondary movements interlock
The circuits of the secondary movements interlock uses a 24 V power supply to control the CON-C
contactor and connect the single-phase power supply to the movement system motors if the
CON-B contactor is energized. The secondary movements interlock makes sure that:
• The BLD touchguards are not operated
• The gantry movement is not operated out of the set limits
• The iViewGT™ touchguard interlock is not operated
• The necessary software interlocks are in their correct conditions for the operation of
the secondary movements interlock.
• The system communications RTUs that control the touchguard, gantry base limit and
software interlock signals are in the correct global state.
During the correct conditions for operation, the circuits that control the secondary movements
interlock keeps the CON-C contactor energized and the contacts closed. If the CON-B contactor is
also energized, there is a 26 V power supply to the movement system motors. If the circuits
operate an interlock, they remove the 24 V power supply and de-energize the CON-C contactor.
The CON-C contacts open and removes the 26 V power supply to the movement system motors.

4.9.9 Operation and analysis of the primary HT interlock (HT1)
The primary HT interlock controls the CON-J contactor. This is one of four contactors in series with
circuit breaker CB1 and the three-phase power supply. All four contactors, and the CB1, must be
energized to make the three-phase power supply available to the HT PSU.
A soft-start circuit decreases the mains voltage for a time before the full three-phase power
supply is made available to the HT PSU.
The primary HT interlock makes sure that:
• The doors in the treatment room and the control room are closed.
• The pressure of the vacuum in the waveguide is correct.
• The TCS do a check of the necessary software conditions to make sure that they have
the correct settings for the operation of HT.
• The collimator, the target shifter, the primary (scatter) filter and the secondary filter
are in the correct position.
• The system communications RTU that controls the HT1 software interlock signals is in
the correct global state.
The HT1 interlock connects the contacts of switches and relays that supply the 24 V AC single-
phase power to the coil of the HT1 CON-J contactor.
When all the HT1 interlock conditions are correct, the CON-J contactor is energized, the contacts
closed, and there is a three-phase power supply to the HT PSU. The operation of an interlock
removes the 24 V AC power supply and de-energize the CON-J contactor. The contacts open and
remove the three-phase power supply to the HT PSU.

The soft start circuit has two resistors, R1 and R3 that are in parallel connection with the contacts
of the CON-D contactor. When the CON-J and the CON-K contactors are energized, resistors
decrease the electrical power supply to the HT PSU. After a short interval, the CON-D contactor
energizes and makes the three-phase mains power supply available to the HT PSU.
The HT soft start PCB, IRC-74A, has a circuit that, in steps, supply increased electrical power to the
HT PSU. The soft start circuit includes the START key on the FKP, the CON-J and the CON-K
contactors. The circuit operates as follows:
1 When you operate the START key on the FKP, the CON-J and the CON-K contactors are
energized and start the HT soft start circuit.
2 After 500 ms the HT soft start circuit times out and supplies +22 V to the coil of relay RL4 on
the PCB IRC-74A.
3 The contacts of RL4 close and supply 24 V to the coil of the CON-D contactor.
4 The contacts of the CON-D contactor close and make the three-phase mains power supply
available to the HT PSU.
A software item, i72, gives the status of the CON-D contactor to the TCS.
See the Digital Accelerator System Diagrams Manual for a diagram of the soft start circuit .

The PRF interlock system has two circuits, PRF A and PRF B. The two circuits can independently
prevent the supply of the PRF from the PPG PCB.
The PRF A circuit has the DIE PCBs and related ROC PCBs in the RTU A in each of the three control
areas. A set of different interlock conditions is applicable to each DIE PCB. When all interlock
conditions are correct, the DIE PCB energizes a relay on the related ROC PCB. When energized,
the contacts of the relays close and send a PRF ENABLE signal through RTU A in each control area
and then to a PPG PCB.

4.9.13 Operation and analysis of the PRF B interlock
The PRF interlock system has two circuits, PRF A and PRF B. The circuits can independently
prevent the supply of the PRF from the PPG PCB.
The PRF B circuit has the DIE PCBs and related ROC PCBs in the RTU B in each of the control areas.
A set of different interlock conditions is applicable to each DIE PCB. When all interlock conditions
are correct, the DIE-PCB energizes a relay on the related ROC PCB. When energized, the contacts
of the relays close and send a PRF ENABLE signal through RTU B in each control area and then to
a PPG PCB.
The conditions of operation for the PRF B interlock are that:
• The RTU B in each control area is in the applicable global state.
• The LT and HT soft-start contactors are in the correct condition.
• The BLD has the correct collimator, target and filter settings.
• The dosimetry channel B is in the correct state.
• The BLD has the correct wedges, filters and electron applicator.
• The pressure of the vacuum at the gun end of the waveguide is correct.
• The temperature of the HT PSU is correct.
• The pressure in the dielectric gas system is correct.
• A diode overload has not occurred.
• The HT2 interlock is enabled.
• The PRF is enabled.
• The TCC emergency shut down relay is closed.

4.9.14.3 Operation of the client system interlocks
The client system interlock circuit includes the EMERGENCY STOP switches and the ON/OFF
switch of the digital accelerator.
Operation of the client system interlock de-energizes the contactor CON-A, which removes the
electrical power supply from:
• The LT system
• The HT system
• The movements system
• The cooling water system.
The ICCA, the vacuum system and the TCS stay energized. These functions operate through the
main interlock.
4.9.14.4 Operation of the room doors interlock
The client interlocks of the customer interface monitor the status of the different room door
switches installed by the hospital. These include:
• Treatment room door switches
• Equipment room door switches
• Neutron door switch
• More customer door switches.
The room door interlocks have two different circuits (1 and 2). The client makes the decision on
the number and positions of the door switches.
The room doors 1 interlock controls the HT1 interlock chain. When the door switches are closed
(safe), the 24 V supply goes through the client interlocks. If a door switch opens (the door open),
the 24 V supply stops and de-energize CON-J.
Item i258 gives the status of the room doors 1 interlock to the TCS through ROC-HTA.
4.9.14.5 Operation of the client beam termination interlock
The beam termination interlock in the client interlock circuit causes a beam termination that is in
compliance with the IEC 60601-2-1 standard. The operation of an external TERMINATE key opens
the HT1 interlock circuit.
One set of contacts on the TERMINATE key connects to pins 9 and 10 on PL73E to show that the
function operated. The input connects directly through IRC-74B, DIE-ICB and ROC-ICB to become
item i30 (external termination). The circuit is usually closed (100Ω), and the contacts open (100
kΩ) and cause a beam termination.

4.9.15 Operation of the interlock relay printed circuit board
The IRC PCB controls the 24 V AC interlock chains in the digital accelerator. Each IRC PCB has
eight, single-pole, relays and a time delay circuit.
Some of the relays are used in the HT1 and HT2 interlock circuits which control the HT contactors.
The two interlock circuits are connected to the client interlocks and in serial with a number of the
relays. Each of the relays is connected to operate in each half of each control area.

5.2 Correcting for an intermittent bending magnet voltage
inhibit
The system is a constant generator of electrical current and increases the voltage to keep the
same current if the PSU senses changes in impedance.
Bad connections can cause local temperature conditions that are too high. Too high a
temperature can change the impedance of the coils. This can then cause changes in the bending
magnet voltage.
The software item i384 inhibits are usually related to:
• Connections of the large positive and negative cables
• Loose nuts, washers or damaged washers put in incorrect sequence
• Loose connections in the connector.

5.3 Correcting an HT overload inhibit
A software inhibit i83 can occur because the thyratron does not switch off in time for the next
charge cycle. Usually the life of the thyratron increases, as the replenisher voltage supplied to it
increases. Therefore if you decrease the thyratron replenisher voltage this decreases the time
that is necessary for the thyratron to switch off. To prevent a HT overload, the replenisher voltage
must be set as high as possible.
WARNING 5.3
Do not do work on the linear accelerator before you isolate the electrical supply to the work
area at the interface cabinet. You can get a high voltage electric shock. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause fatal injury.

5.6 Correcting a general interlock system fault
The interlock system operates when a malfunction of the linear accelerator occurs. This causes
the linear accelerator to stop its operation.
If there is a fault with the interlock system the defective interlock circuit does not operate
correctly. If there is a fault with one interlock, a different interlock always operates. This can make
the faults in the interlock system difficult to find.

5.8 Correcting for a no HT supply fault
There is no HT supply because contactor K (CON-K) does not operate. The linear accelerator gives
an i79 or i72 inhibit.
Note: The contactors CON-J, CON-K and CON-D must be energized to supply a three-phase HT power
supply to the HT PSU.
When the START button is pressed, CON J and CON K energize. This applies a soft start signal to
the HT PSU through the soft start resistors across HT CON D.
The circuit does a check that CON-J and CON-K are closed before a timer on interlock relay card
(IRC-74A) PCB starts. When the hardware timer times out, CON-D is energized. See the Digital
Accelerator System Diagrams Manual.

5.9 Correcting for PRF terminations i607
The PRF termination inhibit (software item i607) occurs at the end of the beam. If the time of the
default start cycle of the segment timer is too short, it can cause a PRF termination error.
The check fail PRF interlock makes sure that the segment timer stops approximately one second
after the dose counter stops. Operation of the check fail PRF interlock adds one second to the
start cycle time of the segment timer. The longer start cycle makes sure that the segment timer
does not operate during field delivery.
Adjust item i43 of part 131 to increase the default time of the start cycle of the segment timer.
The default time of the start cycle is five seconds for FTM linear accelerators and 13 seconds for
Non-FTM linear accelerators.

Make sure that the voltage is between -120 V DC and -160 V DC.

7.3.5.2 Doing a functional test of the excess dose rate interlock
This functional test makes sure that the beam stops if you put in a dose rate that is too high.
There are levels of protection against a high dose-rate that can stop treatment, for example:
• i507 (dose 1 excess rate)
• i513 (dose 1 count not acknowledged)
• i457 (dose 2 excess rate)
• i463 (dose 2 count not acknowledged).
Therefore, in this test, you must increase the gun current at a high rate of change. This will make
sure that only the interlock for this test will operate, not a different interlock.
1 Click Deliver Quick Beam.
2 From the Deliver Quick Beam dialog box, click the Radiation tab.
3 From the Radiation Type list select the type of radiation, for example ELECTRON.
4 From the Energy list, select an energy between 6 MeV and 20 MeV.
5 In Dose Rate box, select a dose rate of 900 MU.
6 Click Load.
7 When the beam is set up correctly, click CONFIRM.
The Beam Delivery box appears.
8 On the FKP, press the MV START key.
9 Make sure that the dose rate is correct, and then set i181 to Manual.
10 Look at i327. Set the value in the left column to the same value as the right column.
11 Increase the gun current in steps of 100 mA to increase the dose rate.
12 Make sure that the beam stops in the range 600 cGy/min to 1200 cGy/min.
The beam stops in a position that is directly related to the rate of dose rate increase.
7.3.5.3 Doing a functional test of the beam timer interlock
The segment timer monitor is a software function. It does not operate through the hardware.
1 Open the Deliver Quick Beam dialog box.
2 Select and load an energy.
3 Set the approved monitor unit (MU) to 1000.
4 Start the beam then halve the dose rate value of i43 on the AFC service page.
5 On the AFC service page, decrease the dose rate value of i43 by half.
6 When the beam timer interlock operates, make sure that the beam stops. The beam will
not stop immediately.
